The Six Pillar Framework (P1-P6): - **P1: Product & Distribution (Design)**: Focus on New Product features, Target Market, and Benefit Design. [Linked Themes: Product Design, Group Products, CI, IP, LTC, PMI, State Healthcare].
- **P2: Pricing & Underwriting (Sell)**: Focus on Assumptions (Morbidity/Mortality), Rating Factors, and Selective Underwriting. [Linked Themes: Pricing, Underwriting].
- **P3: Claims Management (Pay)**: Focus on Fraud control, Triage, TPA management, and Cost containment. [Linked Themes: Claims Management].
- **P4: Risk Mgmt & Reinsurance (Protect)**: Focus on Risk Taxonomy, Reinsurance structures, and SCR/Capital. [Linked Themes: Risks, Reinsurance, Risk Management & Capital].
- **P5: Valuation & Data (Measure)**: Focus on BEL/Risk Margin, IBNR methods, Data quality, and Investments. [Linked Themes: Reserving & Valuation, Data, Models, Investments].
- **P6: Regulation & Ethics (Comply)**: Focus on IRDAI 2024 updates, PPH, and the Appointed Actuary role. [Linked Themes: Regulation, IRDAI Regulations, Professionalism].
Pricing: - **Data & Assumptions**: Experience vs Industry; internal/external credibility; proxies for new products.
- **Demographic Assumptions**: Morbidity/Mortality trends; initial vs selective selection; anti-selection risk.
- **Financial Assumptions**: Risk-free yield curves; medical inflation (~12-15% India); expense loadings (initial/renewal).
- **Risk Profile**: Impact of age, gender, occupation, and geography on claim frequency and severity.
- **Methodology**: Equation of Value (simplistic) vs. Cashflow Projection (dynamic); stochastic vs deterministic.
- **Profitability & Capital**: Target Return on Capital (RoC); Cost of Capital; risk-based capital allocation (SCR).
- **Commercial Constraints**: Competitor benchmarking; sales channel commissions; affordability vs adequacy.
- **Cross-subsidy**: Inter-generational (young subsidizing old) vs Intra-group; regulatory limits on subsidies.
- **Sensitivity & Options**: Stress testing for lapses, medical advances, and option-take up (e.g. renewal guarantees).
Claims Management: - **Notification & Proof**: Clear definitions, documentation required, verification of pre-existing conditions.
- **Assessment / Triage**: Non-disclosure checks, tele-interviews, use of specialized medical officers.
- **Control & Mitigation**: Home visits (IP), second opinions, independent medical exams.
- **Rehabilitation (IP)**: Early intervention, proportionate benefits for partial return, funding treatment to reduce claim duration.
- **Payment / Settlement**: Annuity vs lump sum, tax implications, currency, avoiding over-insurance.
Regulation: - **Key 2024 Updates**: 60-month moratorium (not 48); PED waiting period max 36 months; 1-hr/3-hr cashless TATs; 30-day Free Look; no entry age limits; Board-governed commission via PMC.
- **Key 2024 Updates**: 48-month moratorium; 1-hr/3-hr cashless timelines; 30-day Free Look; removal of entry age limits; Board-governed commission.
- **Prudential Norms**: Available Solvency Margin (ASM) >= 150% of Required Solvency Margin (RSM); statutory reserves certified by AA.
- **State Interaction**: Integration with PMJAY (Ayushman Bharat); public provision defining the floor for private demand.
- **Conduct of Business**: Treating Customers Fairly (TCF) principles; mandatory 15-day grievance resolution via GRM.
- **Capital Frameworks**: Solvency II Pillar 1/2/3 analogues; transition to Risk-Based Capital (RBC) and IFRS 17/Ind AS 117.
- **Taxation**: Section 80D benefits (up to Rs 1L for seniors); 18% GST impact on premiums and affordability.
- **Grievance Redressal**: Ombudsman hierarchy (internal -> Ombudsman -> Consumer Forum -> Civil Court).
- **Professional Roles**: Statutory role of Appointed Actuary (AA); whistleblowing; compliance with IAI APS 33/34.
IRDA Regulations: - **Health Insurance Regulations 2024**: Consolidates and replaces 2016 rules; in force 1 April 2024; PED defined as condition diagnosed/treated within 36 months prior to policy commencement; PED waiting period capped at max 36 months continuous coverage; specific disease waiting period also max 36 months; moratorium = 60 continuous months including portability and migration credits — after which no policy or claim contestable except on grounds of fraud; enhanced sum insured carries fresh 60-month clock; insurer cannot deny renewal on grounds of prior claims; no fresh medical at renewal unless sum insured being increased; grace period 15 days (monthly) / 30 days (other modes); for indemnity policies policyholder chooses primary insurer who coordinates with others for balance amount; for benefit policies claims may be made from all insurers simultaneously; NCB may be offered as cumulative bonus (increase in SI without premium increase) or discount on renewal premium — policyholder chooses form at renewal.

- **Insurance Products Regulations 2024**: Repeals IRDAI (Health Insurance) Regulations 2016, IRDAI (ULIP) Regulations 2019, IRDAI (Non-Linked) Regulations 2019 and others; life insurers may offer benefit basis only (not indemnity); general/health insurers may offer both indemnity and benefit basis; insurer must offer products catering to all ages and all medical conditions including PED, chronic conditions, disabilities, HIV/AIDS, mental illness, and all systems of medicine; AYUSH must be covered at par with allopathy; Board-approved AYUSH policy required; policyholder to have choice of treatment system; premium must remain unchanged during policy term; renewal loadings applied at portfolio level not individual policy level; discounts permitted for good claims experience; File and Use — prior filing to IRDAI and UIN assignment required before launch; Use and File — insurer may launch after UIN assignment without prior IRDAI filing but PMC approval still required.

- **Protection of Policyholders' Interests 2024**: Insurer must decide on proposal form within 7 days of receipt; policy document to be issued within 15 days of acceptance; Free Look period = 30 days from receipt of policy document for policies with term >= 1 year; proportionate premium refund if cancelled during Free Look with no claim; refund processed within 7 days of Free Look cancellation request; delay in refund attracts interest at bank rate + 2% from date of request; mandatory Customer Information Sheet (CIS) with every policy covering sum insured, coverage, exclusions, sub-limits, deductibles, waiting periods, Free Look, portability, moratorium; CIS available in local language on request; no claim repudiated without PMC or 3-member Claims Review Committee (CRC) approval; reason for repudiation must cite specific policy terms; documents to be collected by insurer/TPA — policyholder not required to submit; cashless authorisation within 1 hour; final discharge authorisation within 3 hours; delay beyond 3 hours — additional hospital charges borne by insurer from shareholders' funds; claims not settled within timelines attract interest at bank rate + 2% paid suo-moto by insurer; grievance hierarchy: internal GRM → Insurance Ombudsman → Consumer Forum → Civil Court; Ombudsman award must be complied with within 30 days; non-compliance attracts Rs 5,000 per day penalty to complainant plus penal interest; separate servicing channel required for senior citizen health insurance claims and grievances.

- **Expenses of Management (EOM) 2024**: EOM limit for standalone health insurers = 35% of gross premium written in India per financial year; EOM limit for general insurers = 30% of gross premium written; removal of individual product commission caps; commission and all expenses governed by Board-approved EOM policy reviewed annually; Board policy on commission must specify structure, fairness and reasonableness criteria, good distribution practice principles, conflict of interest safeguards, and compliance monitoring framework; commission must be commensurate with effort to acquire and service business; intermediaries must not recommend products solely to earn higher commission; new commission structures not applicable to in-force policies; IRDAI may exercise forbearance in case of excess EOM subject to conditions; insurer must file Return of EOM in prescribed formats certified by Appointed Actuary.

- **Actuarial, Finance & Investment 2024**: AA eligibility for health insurer: Fellow of IAI; at least 9 years experience in general or health insurance of which at least 4 years post-fellowship (reduced by 2 years if SA3/health specialist subject passed); at least 2 of those post-fellowship years in statutory valuation or product pricing of an Indian general/health insurer; at least 3 years in middle or senior management; full-time employee of insurer; not AA of any other insurer; not over age 70; holds Certificate of Practice from IAI; no business without AA permitted; AA reports directly to CEO; AA must not simultaneously be Chief Risk Officer; health insurer must have at least one additional actuary beyond AA for pricing and valuation; AA duties include: certifying claims reserves (IBNR, IBNER, PDR) using actuarial principles; PDR maintained at insurer level but monitored at segment level; ensuring premium rates are fair; ensuring adequacy of reinsurance; identifying solvency risks and reporting to Board; informing Competent Authority if insurer fails to rectify solvency; certifying assets and liabilities under s.64V; control level of solvency margin = ASM >= 150% RSM at all times; RSM subject to minimum of 50% of minimum capital under s.6 of Insurance Act; negative IBNR for any year of occurrence treated as zero; mathematical reserves cannot fall below surrender value; all policies on bonus distribution, ALM, investment, and risk management must be Board-approved; transition to Risk-Based Capital (RBC) and IFRS 17 / Ind AS 117 framework underway.

- **Re-insurance Regulations 2024**: Simplified Quota Share and Excess of Loss structures; defined retention hierarchies for Indian reinsurers, Foreign Reinsurer Branches (FRBs), and Cross-Border Reinsurers (CBRs); CBR residency and regulatory compliance rules apply; PMC must review reinsurance risks before product launch; Appointed Actuary responsible for ensuring adequacy of reinsurance arrangements; reinsurance arrangements must be consistent with product profitability and capital assessments approved by PMC.

- **Registration & Capital 2024**: Minimum capital entry Rs 100 Cr under s.6 of Insurance Act; structure for amalgamations and transfer of shares; net worth requirements; Board-level governance of all product, expense, and capital decisions via PMC; insurer must at all times maintain ASM >= 150% RSM; IRDAI approval required before AA appointment takes effect; IRDAI must decide on AA appointment application within 30 days of receipt.

- **Appointed Actuary Regulations 2022**: Eligibility: Fellow of IAI; full-time employee; holds Certificate of Practice; not AA of any other insurer; not over 70; no professional misconduct; IRDAI approval required; AA has statutory right to attend and speak at Board, management, shareholder, and policyholder meetings on matters affecting solvency or policyholder interests; no letter of appointment may restrict AA's statutory duties; AA must inform Competent Authority within 7 days of any disciplinary proceedings initiated against him/her; insurer must not obstruct AA in performing duties; AA may not simultaneously be CRO; AA must comply with IAI APS 33/34 and other professional standards; dual reporting obligation to Board and IRDAI.
Risks: ### SP1 Textbook: Nature of Risks (Chapters 23-24)
- **Nature of Risk Components**: Model Risk (inappropriate structure); Parameter Risk (wrong variables used); Random Fluctuations (sample error/heterogeneity).
- **Policy & Other Data Risks**: Inaccurate/incomplete records; Model Point risk (not representing portfolio); External data mismatch (e.g., using USA data for India).
- **Claim Rates (Morbidity/Mortality)**: Future parameters uncertain due to new diseases/trends; 'Expanding Funnel of Doubt' as projections go further.
- **Claim Amounts (PMI specific)**: Short-term variability in procedure costs; medical advances impact (e.g. hip replacement rehab time shifts).
- **Earlier Screening & Diagnosis**: Critical Illness risk—earlier diagnosis = earlier payment; screening prompts claims that might have otherwise lapsed.
- **Investment Performance & Assets**: Capital values risk (snapshot solvency); low reserves in CI/PMI mean low investment importance vs long-term products.
- **Expenses and Inflation**: Mismatch between inflation and loadings; volume impact (low volume = high fixed costs per policy); third-party TPA fees.
- **Persistency & Selective Withdrawal**: Selective effect (healthy leave, substandard remain); amortization risk (initial costs not recouped if early lapse).
- **Mix & Volume of New Business**: Mix by nature/size (smaller policies = lower contribution to fixed costs); Volume risk (High = capital drain; Low = overhead mismatch).
- **Guarantees and Options**: Complexity in terms offered in advance; health events harder to predict than life events; requirement for stochastic models.
- **Competition & Management Actions**: Reduced price/increased coverage to stay competitive; Management may override Actuary's prudence for market share.
- **Counterparties (RI/Distributors/TPAs)**: Reinsurer default; Distributor holding premiums; TPA commitment of expenditure on behalf of insurer.
- **Legal, Regulatory & Tax**: Retrospective changes overriding policy terms; prohibition of certain rating factors (e.g., race/genetics).
- **Reputation & Operational Fraud**: Disrepute via media/social media (especially for sick/elderly); Internal fraud / Audit failure (Embezzlement).
- **Aggregation, Concentration & Cat**: Localized outbreaks (Aggregation); industry concentration (e.g. mining industrial disease); Pandemic/War/Terrorism.
- **Non-disclosure & Anti-selection**: Moratorium approach complications; asymmetric info at proposal; genetic testing debate.
- **Product-specific: IP Insurance**: Sickness transition probabilities (inception/termination); moral hazard (return to work disincentive); leniency of definition.
- **Product-specific: CI Insurance**: Diagnosis rates; limited historical data in new markets; rider vs standalone capital requirements.
- **Product-specific: LTC Insurance**: High claim inception; transfer between dependency levels; investment/reserve significance; marketing risk (expectation of 100% cover).
- **Product-specific: PMI**: Third-party control over claims (consultants/doctors); single large claims vs accumulation; low capital strain vs long-term.
Underwriting: - **Objective**: Prevent anti-selection, classify risks equitably, maintain competitor parity.
- **Alternative Approaches**: Moratorium underwriting (often PMI), Free Cover Limits (Group).
- **Outcomes**: Standard rates, rated premiums (+%), exclusions, deferral, decline.
- **Operational**: Cost vs. Value of info, speed of onboarding (straight-through processing).
Risk Management & Capital: - **Quantification**: Stress and Scenario testing, Internal Models vs. Standard Formula.
- **Mitigation**: Reinsurance, Asset-Liability Matching (ALM), product redesign (reviewable premiums), stricter claims control.
- **Capital**: Economic capital vs. Regulatory capital, risk appetite statements, diversification benefits.
Reserving & Valuation: - **Components**: Mathematical reserves (BEL), Risk Margin, Unearned Premium Reserve (UPR), Outstanding Claims (OSLR), IBNR.
- **Methods**: Bornhuetter-Ferguson, Chain Ladder / Run-off triangles (short-tailed), Cashflow projection (long-tailed).
- **Differences**: Pricing (realistic + profit margin) vs. Supervisory (prudent/margins) vs. Embedded Value (market consistent).
- **Prudence**: Allowance for adverse deviation, discounting at risk-free rates.
Reinsurance: ### 1. Reasons for Reinsurance

- **Limitation of Exposure to Risk**: Factors affecting appetite include size of insurer, marketplace experience, available free assets, portfolio size (credibility), and predictability of business outcome. Larger free assets and larger portfolio reduce the need for reinsurance.

- **Avoidance of Large Single Losses**: Insurer compares potential maximum claim to free assets. Small/medium insurers cede top slice of large payouts to protect solvency. Particularly relevant for PMI where individual claims can reach hundreds of thousands.

- **Smoothing of Results**: Reinsurance reduces variance of expected experience relative to mean. Helps achieve smooth year-on-year accounts, especially for immature portfolios. Stop loss directly reduces extreme losses. Nearly all reinsurance types smooth profits to some degree.

- **Availability of Expertise**: When entering new risk areas, reinsurer assists with product design, pricing, underwriting and claims management. Often via quota share with low initial retention. As insurer gains experience and confidence, reinsurer involvement reduces.

- **Increasing Capacity to Accept Risk**: Insufficient capital may prevent accepting certain risks by sector or volume. Reinsurance cover reduces solvency capital requirements in line with proportion ceded (subject to upper limit due to reinsurer default risk).

- **Financial Assistance**: (i) New business strain: reinsurance commission factors future surplus streams as an effective loan. (ii) Improving free assets: reinsurer pays initial commission, receives share of future surpluses; arrangement is off-balance-sheet if contingent. (iii) Merger/acquisition: reinsurer advances funds against identified profitable block. Quota share is the norm for all three.

- **Arbitrage**: Solvency capital arbitrage: reinsurer holds less capital per unit of risk, passes saving to cedant via lower premiums. Tax arbitrage: reinsurer subject to less onerous taxes, enabling favourable reinsurance premium rates.
### 2. Types of Reinsurance Business

- **Facultative**: Individual risk-by-risk negotiation. Cedant free to place with any reinsurer; reinsurer free to accept or reject. Used for risks outside treaty scope (unusually large or unusual underwriting features). Disadvantages: time-consuming, no certainty of cover, possible delay in accepting risk.

- **Treaty (Obligatory/Obligatory)**: Formal obligatory arrangement covering a defined class. Efficient (automatic placement), certain (cover guaranteed within limits), but inflexible. Treaty must precisely define: scope of cover, financial arrangements (premiums, commissions, timing), and obligations of both parties.

- **Proportional - Quota Share**: Constant proportion R% of every risk ceded. Applies to long-term (original terms or risk premium basis) and short-term (original terms only). Administratively simple. Purposes: spread risk, write larger portfolios, encourage reciprocal business, improve solvency ratio, finance new business strain. Disadvantage: cedes same proportion regardless of size; passes share of profit to reinsurer.

- **Proportional - Surplus**: Only for long-term business with fixed sum insured (not applicable to PMI). Insurer sets a monetary retention; sum insured above retention is ceded. Proportion ceded varies by policy size. More efficient than quota share at targeting morbidity risk. Disadvantage: less control over parameter risk; less suitable for financing arrangements.

- **Original Terms (Coinsurance)**: Premium and claims split in same fixed proportion. Reinsurer shares all risks of policy including investment and lapse risk. Reinsurer pays commission to cedant to cover initial expenses. Deposits back: reinsurer deposits its reserve share with cedant, retaining no investment risk; also mitigates reinsurer default risk.

- **Risk Premium Reinsurance**: Reinsurer sets its own premium rate independently of the cedant's premium. Gives cedant freedom to reprice without changing reinsurance terms. Premiums applied to sum reinsured (proportion of sum insured or sum at risk). Can be level (net level premium) or yearly increasing. Rates may be guaranteed or reviewable. Profit participation possible.

- **Sum-at-Risk Reinsurance**: Proportions applied to the excess of benefit over reserve (not full sum insured). Only useful where benefit is a lump sum and reserves are large enough to be significant. Not usually used for IP (total benefit unknown) or typical CI (reserves too small). Exception: unit-linked CI where sum at risk = benefit minus bid value of units.

- **Non-Proportional - Risk XL**: Covers individual losses above an excess point up to an upper limit. Multiple layers stack with no gaps between them. Expressed as X in excess of Y. Primarily found on PMI portfolios. Stability clause may index limits to inflation. Commission not applicable (profit commission possible on lower layers). Proportional-within-non-proportional (e.g. 90% of layer) used to mitigate moral hazard in claims settlement.

- **Non-Proportional - Aggregate XL / Stop Loss**: Covers aggregation of multiple claims over a 12-month period. Excess point and upper limit expressed as percentage of earned premium (e.g. 110% to 130% claims ratio). In health insurance, aggregate XL and stop loss are interchangeable (only peril is morbidity). Reinsurers reluctant to provide due to limited underwriting control and historically loss-making experience. Conditions imposed: deductible; control over underwriting and claims.

- **Non-Proportional - Catastrophe XL**: Covers claims from a single event (chemical explosion, epidemic, pandemic). Individual claims may not breach the excess point but their aggregate from one event does. Pandemic-specific cover rarely available at affordable price. Government may intervene in severe pandemic events.

- **Financial Reinsurance (Fin Re)**: Minimal insurance risk transfer. Primary aim: improve apparent accounting or supervisory solvency of cedant. (i) Risk premium method: reinsurer pays large upfront commission (effective loan), cedant repays via inflated future risk premiums. (ii) Contingent loan (surplus relief): reinsurer lends against future VIF profits; repayment contingent on profits emerging so not reserved as liability, improving free asset position without increasing liabilities. Not effective under Solvency II or realistic reporting regimes.
### 3. Determination of Retention Level

- **Stochastic Modelling Approach**: Estimate distribution of risk experience costs at various retention levels. Use simulation to find retention where insolvency probability is below a specified threshold (e.g. solvent in 995 out of 1,000 runs). Long-term fixed benefit contracts: model claim rates stochastically. Short-term indemnity contracts (PMI): model both claim incidence rates AND claim amounts stochastically.

- **Cost Minimisation Approach**: Balance (a) cost of financing a risk experience fluctuation reserve and (b) cost of reinsurance including expense/profit loadings. As retention rises, (a) increases and (b) decreases. Choose retention that minimises total of (a) + (b).

- **Factors Driving Lower Retention (More Reinsurance)**: Greater uncertainty about future claims experience; lower acceptable insolvency probability; greater variance of benefit level distribution. Large insurers with sufficient free assets may dispense with XL entirely except for exceptionally large policies and catastrophes.
### 4. Key Examiner Watchpoints
- **Reinsurer Default Risk**: Even if fully reinsured, cedant remains liable to policyholders. A minimum capital level must always be held. Deposit back arrangements mitigate this risk.

- **When Reinsurer Writes Loss-Making Business**: Expects higher future profits from the business; profits from other connected business (same cedant or reinsurance broker); or retaining market share at the bottom of the reinsurance cycle when premiums are low across the market.

- **Quota Share vs Surplus Comparison**: Quota share applies same percentage to all risks (simple, good for financing and solvency improvement). Surplus applies variable percentage by policy size (efficient for targeting large risks, reduces concentration risk). Surplus not suitable for PMI as benefit amount is unknown.

- **Stop Loss Quoted as Percentage of Premium Income**: Prevents insurer from writing more volume to trigger fixed monetary limits; ensures limits and premium charged rise proportionally with business written.

- **Why Insurer Handles XL Claims Prudently Even If 100% Reinsured Above Excess**: Maintains long-term relationship with reinsurer; potential profit commission at risk; future reinsurance premiums related to past claims experience.
Product Design: ### Product Design Framework (SP1 Chapters 1-5)
#### Core Definition & Scope

- **Product Types by Duration**: Long-term (IP, CI, LTCI: typically 5+ years, usually no renewal) vs Short-term (PMI, health cash plans: annual renewable). Long-term products often sold by life insurers; short-term by general insurers or specialists.

- **Scope Coverage (Must Precisely Define)**: For PMI—Inpatient (IP), Outpatient (OP), Day care, Domiciliary, AYUSH. Each must be explicitly defined. OP cover has much higher frequency/lower severity than IP; major operational readiness required before launch.

- **Benefit Type & Risk Bearer**: Indemnity (insurer bears medical inflation risk; reimburses actual costs) vs Fixed Benefit/Benefit Basis (insurer knows max exposure; policyholder bears cost variation risk). Life insurers in India restricted to benefit basis only; general/health insurers offer both.
#### Target Market & Customer Needs

- **Market Segmentation**: By age, income, occupation, health status (including pre-existing conditions). Regulatory mandate (IRDAI 2024): must offer products catering to all ages, medical conditions, PED, chronic illness, disabilities, HIV/AIDS, mental illness, and all treatment systems (allopathy, AYUSH).
- **Gap Analysis vs State Provision**: Products designed to fill gaps left by PMJAY (Ayushman Bharat—covers bottom 40% income). Private PMI adds depth of cover, wider hospital choice, faster access.
- **Needs Assessment**: Match benefit design to policyholder protection gaps; distinguish between affordability (what they can pay) vs adequacy (what they actually need).
#### Benefit & Coverage Design

- **Benefit Limits & Sub-limits**: Sum insured (global); sub-limits by service (room, ICU, surgery, consultants, organ transplants, mental illness parity). Room/board limits create incentive toward cost-effective hospitals. Mental illness parity mandated—difficult to diagnose/control, fraud risk.
- **Waiting Periods (IRDAI 2024 Limits)**:
- PED waiting period: maximum 36 months continuous coverage (was 48 months)
- Disease-specific waiting period: maximum 36 months
- Moratorium (non-contestability): 60 continuous months; after which no denial except fraud. Moratorium clock includes portability/migration credits, resets on enhanced sum insured.

- **No Claim Discount (NCD)**: Offered as cumulative bonus (increase in SI without premium increase) or discount on renewal premium—policyholder chooses form. Healthy lives self-select for NCD; pricing must account for selection benefit.
- **Sum Insured Reinstatement**: Applies to new illnesses only (not pre-existing/PED). Annual or per-claim reinstatement; critical design decision affecting benefit adequacy and claims frequency.

- **Co-payments, Deductibles, Co-insurance**: Cost-sharing mechanisms (reduce moral hazard; increase policyholder engagement). Regulatory compliance: must be clearly disclosed in Customer Information Sheet (CIS).
#### Product Features & Options

- **Key Features for PMI/IP**: Second opinion provision, home nursing, day-care procedures, domiciliary care, ambulance cover, pre-authorization (mandatory under IRDAI 2024; 1-hour TAT for cashless), cashless facility.
- **Guarantees & Options**:
- Guaranteed Issue Options (GIO): No medical underwriting at entry; attracts anti-selection; premium loadings required.
- Conversion Options: Rider-to-standalone conversion; benefit enhancement without fresh medical.
- Guaranteed Renewability: Premium reviewable at portfolio level (not individual policy) per IRDAI 2024; no denial of renewal on grounds of prior claims.
- Premium Guarantee Period: 1 or 3-year guarantee typical; insurer cannot reprice individual policies within guarantee period.

- **Surrender/Discontinuance Values**: Usually nil for indemnity PMI/CI (no savings element). Impact model pricing as policyholder can exit without penalty (lapse risk). LTCI may have return-of-premium rider.
#### Exclusions & Conditions (Must Be Clearly Defined)

- **Standard Medical Exclusions**: War, terrorism, non-disclosure, criminal acts, cosmetic procedures (except post-accident), fertility treatments, experimental/unproven treatments, sports injuries (depending on policy), alcohol/drug-related claims, self-inflicted injury.
- **Occupational/Hazard Exclusions**: High-risk occupations (mining, aviation, hazmat); extreme sports; geographic restrictions (war zones, epidemiology reasons).
- **Territorial Limits**: World-wide, Asia-Pacific, or India-only. Affects premium; claims management capability in overseas locations.
- **Pre-existing Disease (PED) Definition (IRDAI 2024)**: Any condition diagnosed/treated within 36 months prior to policy commencement. Clear definition essential to avoid disputes.

- **Waiting Periods**: General waiting period (usually 30 days for acute conditions), disease-specific (36 months for chronic disease), maternity (usually 9 months), PED (36 months max). Clearly stated in policy document.
#### Operational & Marketability Constraints

- **Platform Capacity**: Product design must match insurer's IT/underwriting/claims processing systems. Complex benefits require robust pre-authorization and adjudication engines. OP cover with high volume demands high-capacity, low-cost processing.

- **Distribution Channel Alignment**: Distribution channel (direct, tied agents, brokers, group) influences commission structure, underwriting standards, claims management approach, and product positioning.

- **Reinsurance Treaty Scope**: Product design must fit within reinsured limits; excess of policy limits over reinsurance creates unhedged exposure. Quota share, XL layers, and aggregate XL affect product feasibility.

- **Market Positioning & Saleability**: Brand positioning (economy, standard, premium); perceived value vs price; comparative advantages vs competitors. 'Saleability' vs 'adequacy' tension: complex, comprehensive cover is hard to sell; simple cover may be inadequate.
- **Bundling & Cross-Selling**: CI/IP riders attached to life cover increase attachment rate but complicate experience analysis and claims management.
#### Risks & Design Mitigations

- **Moral Hazard (Over-Insurance)**: Risk that policyholder deliberately incurs claims or inflates claims. Mitigation: benefit limits, co-payments, replacement ratio caps, claims review, hospital networks with fixed tariffs.
- **Anti-Selection (Adverse Selection)**:
- At Entry: Healthy lives opt for policies; comprehensive cover attracts sicker lives. Mitigation: appropriate medical underwriting, waiting periods, exclusions.
- At Renewal: Selective lapsing (healthy lives leave when premiums rise; sicker remain). Mitigation: competitive pricing, strong retention strategy, NCD design to reward persistence.
- NCD Selection: Healthier lives self-select for NCD; pricing must account for improved risk pool selecting into NCD.

- **Complexity & Mis-Selling Risk**: Complex exclusions, sub-limits, waiting periods lead to policyholder dissatisfaction and complaints. Mitigation: clear wording, mandatory CIS, simplified benefit designs, staff training.

- **Medical Inflation Risk**: In indemnity PMI, insurer bears medical inflation (~12-15% p.a. in India historically; varies by treatment type). Failure to trend assumptions forward leads to rapid deterioration. Mitigation: annual repricing, inflation-linked sub-limits, shift toward benefit basis.
#### Profitability & Capital Impact (Solvency II Pillar 1)

- **Capital Strain**: New product launches consume capital due to negative asset shares early in policy life (acquisition costs exceed first-year premiums). Affects profit targets and ROI calculations.
- **Return Metrics**: Target Return on Capital (RoC) by product; Value of New Business (VNB); Embedded Value (EV). Hurdle rate typically 15-25% depending on product and company risk appetite.

- **Solvency Capital Requirement (SCR) Impact**: New products with high claims uncertainty, guarantees, or policyholder options increase SCR. Must be assessed before launch via capital projection models.
- **Regulatory Capital (IRDAI 2024)**: Available Solvency Margin >= 150% Required Solvency Margin; affects distribution of free assets and capital-raising capacity.
#### India-Specific Regulatory & Market Context (IRDAI 2024)

- **Regulatory Mandates**: IRDAI 2024 requires Board approval of all product, pricing, expense, and capital decisions via Product Management Committee (PMC). File-and-Use or Use-and-File regime for new products; UIN assignment required before launch.

- **Portability & Migration**: All credits (SI, NCB, waiting periods, moratorium) transfer to new insurer; existing insurer provides data within 72 hours via Insurance Information Bureau (IIB). Acquiring insurer decides within 5 days. Alters experience significantly as waiting periods do not re-apply.

- **PMJAY Integration**: Government's Ayushman Bharat program covers bottom 40% with basic indemnity cover. Private insurers fill gap for middle/upper income; higher sum insured, wider hospital choice, faster claim settlement.

- **ABHA (Ayushman Bharat Health Account) & Digital Records**: National health account system improves UW and fraud detection capability. Voluntary participation; uptake still low. Data privacy regulations (DPDP 2023) restrict usage.
- **Medical Inflation Risk**: Historical trend ~12-15% p.a. in India (varies: ICU ~20%, routine OP ~8%). Repricing essential annually but risks selective lapsing if competitor pricing lags.

- **Regulatory Compliance Costs**: Detailed CIS, 30-day Free Look, mandatory 15-day grievance resolution, Board-governed commission, Treating Customers Fairly (TCF) principles, Ombudsman interface. Increase operational expenses and slow claims settlement.
#### Product-Specific Design Considerations

- **IP Insurance**: Key levers—Deferred Period (DP: 4, 8, 13, 26 weeks), Replacement Ratio (capped max 50-75% to maintain work incentive), Definition (Own Occ vs Any Occ vs ADL). Moral hazard higher in economic downturns ('unemployment disguise'). Group IP easier to underwrite (active-at-work condition).

- **CI Insurance**: Fixed lump sum on diagnosis (not on claim payment). Survival period (typically 14-28 days) reduces frequency but increases severity. Standalone vs accelerated (rider). Diagnosis-based (pays on diagnosis) vs cash plan (pays after diagnosis period). High anti-selection risk; medical advances change diagnosis timing.

- **LTCI**: Long claims duration (average 3-5 years); high inception; significant reserve build. Benefit-triggered on ADL loss (activities of daily living). Transfer between dependency levels (independent → partial → full). Savings element justifies surrender value design.

- **PMI**: Annual renewable; multiple claims per policy. Indemnity or benefit basis. NCD critical for persistency. Pre-authorization reduces moral hazard; cashless settlement (100% target under IRDAI 2024) reduces administration. Provider networks essential for cost control.
Data: - **Data Issues**: Lack of volume, heterogeneity, changes in internal processes, coding errors, lags in reporting.
- **Solutions**: Reinsurer data, Population proxies (adjusted), Grouping data.
- **Data Quality**: Accuracy, completeness, consistency, timeliness (TAS 100 / AS 34).
Models: - **Model Choice**: Multi-state (Markov) vs. Formula/Equation of Value. Deterministic vs Stochastic.
- **Governance**: Peer review, backtesting against actual experience, sensitivity testing.
- **Parameters**: Best estimate vs. Prudent, allowance for medical inflation and longevity.
Group Products: ### Group Health & Care Insurance Framework (SP1 Chapter 5)
#### Definition & Structure
- **Group Definition**: Collection of individuals combining to make single proposal for uniform insurance cover.
- **Key Requirements**: (1) Eligibility definition, (2) Clearly defined benefits by size/definition/period, (3) Uniform cover terms.
- **Common Sponsor**: Employer; often pays part/all premium. Can be affinity group, club membership, credit card linked.
#### Purposes of Group Insurance
- **Legal Requirements**: In some territories, IP insurance compulsory for employers.
- **Recruitment/Retention**: Positive message to prospective/existing employees; competitive benefit.
- **Target Flexibility**: Can appeal to all employees or selected groups (e.g., managers only).
- **Health & Return-to-Work Promotion**: Encourages health management; facilitates speedy return following operation.
- **Smooth Early Retirement**: IP insurance mechanism for managing early retirement of unhealthy employees.
- **Tax Advantages**: Employer contribution may be tax-deductible; employee benefit may have tax-favourable treatment.
- **Perceived Value**: Benefit perceived as worth more to employee than cost to employer (tax efficiency).
#### Flexible Benefit Schemes (Cafeteria Benefits)
- **Structure**: Core benefits + employee choice from menu of alternatives (some requiring staff contribution).
- **Advantages**: Employees select benefits matching personal needs; greater flexibility.
- **Insurer Challenges**: Anti-selection risk (members choose benefits they expect to claim); increased administration; difficulty determining basic risk costs.
- **Tax Implications**: Depend on benefit type and jurisdiction; some benefits treated as taxable salary.
#### Underwriting & Acceptance
- **Use of Average Assumptions**: Underwriter assesses group on average; some poor health lives accepted if balanced by excellent health lives.
- **Statistical Credibility**: Group's experience used to derive premiums if sufficient volume credibility; can accept lives without significant portfolio changes.
- **Special Considerations**: May assess IP/PMI/CI claims in payment or about to be paid (liability pass to new insurer); critical for scheme takeovers.
- **Scheme Takeover**: When employer moves group to new insurer, new insurer may assume current + future liabilities; detailed assessment of current claims needed.
#### Anti-Selection Controls
- **Restrictions**:
- Applying exclusions
- Setting free cover limits (IP/CI only, not PMI)
- Ensuring 'actively at work' qualification
- Setting take-up rates on voluntary schemes
- Laying down take-over terms from previous insurer
- **Lower Anti-Selection Schemes**: Minimum member numbers, compulsory membership, benefit formula linked to salary. Result: more generous free cover limits, smaller premium loadings.
- **Extent Varies**: Anti-selection typically lower for groups than individuals; depends on acceptance rules and flexible benefit structure.
#### Free Cover (Non-Selection Limits)
- **Definition**: Amount of cover automatically granted without underwriting to group members. Only those exceeding limit need medical underwriting.
- **Applicability**: Group IP and CI only (not PMI, as indemnity-based benefit amount unknown until claim).
- **Purpose**: Reduce UW cost; avoid inconvenience of mass medical screening.
- **Eligibility**: Typically for large groups (sufficient risk units that no individual is significant); compulsory membership usual requirement.
- **Formula Design**: Aimed to enable most lives in most schemes to be accepted without UW, provided compulsory membership. Higher-paid employees likely exceed limit (need individual UW).
- **Credibility & Scheme Size**: Lower free cover levels on smaller schemes (avoid single member carrying large proportion of total risk); larger schemes support higher limits.
- **Actively at Work Requirement**: Vital to success of non-underwritten cover. Employer recruitment process achieves health screening rather than explicit insurer UW.
- Example: Employee must have worked continuously preceding 2 months at cover start.
- **Admission After Absence**: Special rules for members failing 'actively at work' qualification (off sick); can return to scheme once requirements re-met.
#### Group IP Insurance Specifics (Chapter 5)
- **Benefit Level**: Gross salary basis (simpler admin) or net pay basis (up to 90% net pre-disability income).
- **Additional Benefits**: Employee/employer pension contributions, State welfare contributions.
- **Benefit Escalation**: While in claim, benefits escalate to maintain claimant's standard of living.
- **Continuation Option**: Employees leaving employment can convert to individual cover without health evidence; limited to group benefit levels. Anti-selection risk high.
- **Statutory Sick Pay**: Employers insure liability to pay statutory sick pay (e.g., UK: 4 days–28 weeks). Common 28-week DP matches statutory period.
- **Discretionary Sick Pay**: Group IP can cover sick pay after statutory period; employer discretionary top-up.
#### Group CI Insurance Specifics
- **Free Cover Limit**: Appropriate (members have varying sum insured, usually salary multiple; some over limit need UW).
- **Benefit Levels**: Usually salary-based multiples (e.g., 2-5x salary).
- **Active at Work**: Compulsory for free cover schemes.
#### Group PMI Specifics
- **Free Cover**: NOT applicable (indemnity-based; actual benefit unknown until claim). Concept of FCL does not work.
- **Alternative Tiering**: Budget vs comprehensive cover; comprehensive underwritten, budget not. Not called 'free cover' in practice.
- **Rate-at-Risk vs Pooled Renewal**: Renewal terms can be rate-at-risk (each group renewed at own experience) or pooled (experience spreads across groups).
- **Large Group Advantages**: Groups 1000+ offer scale benefits; better pricing, more flexibility.
#### Pricing Methodology (Group Products)
- **Experience Rating**: Based on group's past claims experience; more credible with size.
- **Community Rating**: Groups without sufficient experience use community (territory/occupation) rates.
- **Rate Guarantees**: 1 or 3-year guarantees typical; premium fixed during guarantee period.
- **Portfolio-Level Pricing**: IRDAI 2024: renewal loadings applied at portfolio level, not individual policy level.
#### Distribution & Administration
- **Employer Role**: Sponsor; may facilitate payroll deduction; communicates to employees.
- **Individual Underwriting**: Varies by scheme structure; free cover reduces UW burden.
- **Claims Handling**: Can support return-to-work initiatives; employer partnership on rehabilitation.
Investments: - **Nature of Liabilities**: Currency, term/duration, fixed vs real (inflation-linked).
- **Matching**: Match duration to immunize against yield curve shifts. Real assets (Equities/Property) for inflation, fixed-interest for non-linked.
- **Constraints**: Regulatory limitations on asset classes, Liquidity needs (PMI claims are sudden), Solvency II capital charges on risky assets.
Professional Guidance: - **Standards**: Following APS/TAS (e.g. TAS 100), ensuring advice is clear, comprehensive, and unbiased.
- **Role**: Statutory duties (Chief Actuary / With-Profits Actuary), Peer review requirements.
- **Ethics**: Whistleblowing, managing conflicts of interest, handling data quality issues responsibly.
Capital & Solvency: - **Role of Capital**: Provide confidence to policyholders and regulators, buffer against shocks.
- **Solvency II**: Pillar 1 (Quantitative: BEL, Risk Margin, SCR), Pillar 2 (Qualitative: ORSA, Governance), Pillar 3 (Disclosure).
- **Management**: Optimizing capital via Reinsurance or ALM, Return on Capital (ROC) thresholds.
Private Medical Insurance (PMI): ### Private Medical Insurance (PMI) Framework (SP1 Chapter 4)
#### Definition & Nature
- **Core Purpose**: Indemnity-based product reimbursing cost of private medical treatment. Short-term, annual renewable.
- **Alternative Basis**: Fixed benefit/cash plan (insurer knows max exposure; policyholder bears cost variation).
- **Related Products**: Health cash plans, dental/optical plans, major medical expenses, waiting-list plans.
#### Product Scope & Coverage
- **Coverage Areas** (must be explicitly defined): Inpatient (IP), Outpatient (OP), Day care, Domiciliary, AYUSH (all systems equal per IRDAI 2024).
- **Operational Complexity**: OP cover has much higher frequency/lower severity than IP. Major operational readiness required before OP launch (high-volume, low-value processing).
- **Indemnity vs Benefit Basis**: Life insurers in India restricted to benefit basis only; general/health insurers offer both.
#### Key Product Features

- **No Claim Discount (NCD)**: Offered as cumulative bonus (increased SI without premium increase) or renewal premium discount. Policyholder chooses at renewal. Self-selection by healthy lives; pricing must account for selection benefit.
- **Sum Insured Reinstatement**: Applies to new illnesses only (not pre-existing); annual or per-claim basis. Critical design choice affecting benefit adequacy/claim frequency.
- **Sub-Limits by Service**: Room/board limits, surgery limits, ICU limits, consultant limits, organ transplant limits. Room limits incentivize cost-effective hospitals.
- **Co-Payments, Deductibles, Co-Insurance**: Cost-sharing (reduce moral hazard; increase policyholder engagement). Must be clearly disclosed in CIS.
- **Additional Benefits**: Second opinion, home nursing, day-care procedures, domiciliary care, ambulance, pre-authorisation (mandatory 1-hour TAT under IRDAI 2024).
- **Waiting Periods** (IRDAI 2024 limits):
- PED waiting period: max 36 months continuous coverage
- Disease-specific waiting period: max 36 months
- General waiting period: typically 30 days (acute conditions)
- No waiting on accident-related claims (cash plans)
- **Mental Illness Parity**: IRDAI 2024 mandated; difficult to diagnose/control; higher fraud risk.
#### Underwriting & Anti-Selection
- **Full Medical Underwriting (FMU)**: Reduces adverse selection; high admin cost. Balance evidence cost vs experience benefit.
- **Moratorium Approach**: Simpler but risks claim disputes post-60-month moratorium (non-contestable except fraud).
- **Community Rating**: Cross-subsidy across ages; requires open enrolment + risk equalisation to prevent adverse selection spiral.
- **Age-Banding**: Intermediate approach; balance community spirit with experience-rated reality.
- **Relaxed UW for 50+**: Attracts adverse selection; if first-mover without premium adjustment, competitors cherry-pick healthier lives.
- **Front-End UW (India Standard)**: Detailed medical assessment at proposal; clearer policy position; explicit exclusions avoid later disputes.
- **Anti-Selection Controls**: Waiting periods, PED exclusions, pre-authorisation (controls moral hazard, ensures care pathway).
#### Pricing Framework
- **Medical Inflation**: Key risk (~12-15% p.a. in India historically; varies by treatment type). Super-inflation trend observed. Failure to trend forward leads to rapid deterioration.
- **Burning Cost Methodology**: Frequency × Severity; split by IP/OP, age, gender, duration, UW method, channel.
- **Claims Deterioration Causes**: Adverse mix, anti-selection, moral hazard, medical inflation, new treatments, poor persistency, aggressive initial pricing.
- **A/E Analysis at Renewal**: Decompose frequency/severity/mix vs assumptions; sensitivity test; care to avoid adverse selection spiral.
- **NCD Pricing**: Healthy lives selecting NCD are better risks; model take-up, selection benefit, behavioural response.
- **Repricing Risks**: Annual repricing essential but risks selective lapsing if competitor pricing lags; competitor response analysis critical.
#### Claims Management & Cost Control
- **Pre-Authorisation**: Controls moral hazard; ensures care pathway. Mandatory 1-hour TAT (IRDAI 2024).
- **Provider Networks**: Agreed tariffs; cashless settlement. 100% cashless target (IRDAI 2024).

- **Capitation vs Fee-for-Service vs Bundled**: Capitation (fixed per covered life; provider bears utilisation risk) harder to negotiate except OP/dental. Fee-for-service (insurer bears utilisation risk). Bundled payment (procedure bundles).
- **Cost Containment Levers**: Day clinics, second opinion (reduces unnecessary procedures), benefit limits (room, surgery, chronic), chronic disease management, wellness incentives.
- **High Complaint Volume**: Characteristic of PMI; strict requirement (no claim repudiated without PMC approval or 3-member CRC; reason must cite specific policy terms).
- **OP Claims Processing**: Robust high-volume, low-value systems essential. Different underwriting/settlement approach vs IP (lump sum vs multiple small claims).
#### Market Dynamics & Distribution
- **New Entrant Risks**: No UW/claims expertise, competitive pricing pressure, high fixed expenses, pricing uncertainty, adverse selection spiral risk.
- **Poor Experience Investigations**: Split by channel, age, gender, UW method, duration. A/E on frequency/severity/persistency; expense analysis; check UW standards; review reinsurer recoveries.
- **Actions on Poor Experience**: Revise UW, expense control, redesign benefits, reprice, improve claims control, change target market, run-off or sale.
- **Digital Distribution**: Attracts younger, healthier lives; different risk profile than traditional broker/agent channels.
- **AI-Based UW**: Regulatory concerns; IRDAI compliance required; transparency/fairness challenges.
- **Group PMI**: FCL avoids individual UW; rate-at-risk vs pooled renewal; large groups (1000+) offer scale benefits.
#### India-Specific Context (IRDAI 2024)

- **Portability & Crediting**: All credits (SI, NCB, waiting periods, moratorium) transfer to new insurer; 72-hour data transfer via IIB. Acquiring insurer decides within 5 days. Alters experience significantly (waiting periods do not re-apply).
- **SAHIs (Standalone Health Insurers)**: Can offer both indemnity and benefit products (life insurers: benefit basis only).
- **Mental Illness Parity**: Mandated at par with physical illness; difficult to diagnose, fraud risk, higher frequency.
- **PMJAY Integration**: Ayushman Bharat covers bottom 40% with basic indemnity. Private PMI fills gap for middle/upper income: higher sum, wider choice, faster settlement.
- **ABHA (Electronic Health Records)**: Improves UW and fraud detection; voluntary; uptake low; data privacy risk (DPDP 2023).
- **Medical Inflation & Repricing**: Repricing risks selective lapsing but essential; climate risks affecting healthcare costs.

- **Regulatory Costs**: Detailed CIS, 30-day Free Look, mandatory 15-day grievance resolution, Board-governed commission, TCF principles, Ombudsman interface, 1-hr/3-hr TAT, no claim denial without CRC approval.
#### Related Products
- **Waiting-List Plans**: Cover only when public healthcare cannot provide treatment within specified period (e.g., 6 weeks). Meets customer need to avoid waiting.
- **Health Cash Plans**: Defined-benefit, defined-premium; low premium (~$2/week); specific payouts for dental, optical, physio, maternity, hospitalisation, recuperation, hearing aids, consultation.

- **Major Medical Expenses (MME)**: Lump-sum benefit on surgery (fixed schedule by procedure class/severity); less than indemnity premium. UK example: covers in-patient costs + incidentals. Does not cover out-patient.
Critical Illness: ### Critical Illness (CI) Insurance Framework (SP1 Chapter 2)
#### Definition & Nature
- **Product Name Variants**: Dread disease insurance, serious illness insurance, crisis cash, living assurance. All refer to critical illness insurance (CI).

- **Core Benefit Structure**: Lump sum payment typically payable on diagnosis of defined conditions. Payment can be in instalments (reduces fraud risk) or used to purchase impaired-life annuity for regular income.

- **Not Indemnity**: Sum insured chosen by policyholder independently—not designed to return policyholder to pre-condition position. Unlike IP (addresses income loss) or PMI (reimburses medical costs).
- **Pure Protection Product**: No guarantee sum insured will be paid (not certain that insured events occur). No surrender value, no maturity benefit. Policy ends on first claim or at term end.
#### Product Variations & Structures
- **Standalone CI**: Sum insured paid only on diagnosis of insured condition; no payment on death.
- **Accelerated CI (Rider on Term Life)**: Sum insured paid on diagnosis of insured CI or death, whichever occurs first. Death benefit reduced by any CI payment made.

- **Benefit Payment Methods**: (1) Lump sum (event-based, independent of severity), (2) Degree of Impairment (e.g., loss of ability to walk unaided), (3) Surgical Procedure (e.g., major organ transplant, heart bypass).
- **Hybrid Products**: Installment option with outstanding amount payable on death; can help claimant manage finances; reduces fraud potential.
#### Characteristics of Insurable CI Conditions
- **Three Core Requirements**:

1. **Perceived Serious & Occurs Frequently**: Public perception drives coverage (sometimes disproportionate to actual incidence). 'Lifestyle-threatening' if not life-threatening. Must not be so rare as to be negligible.

2. **Clearly Defined**: Avoid ambiguity at claim time. Use precise medical terminology (e.g., cancers restricted to malignant tumours invading adjacent tissue; heart attacks require enzyme changes in blood). Prevents windfall payments for minor conditions.

3. **Sufficient Pricing Data**: Must predict future incidence accurately; difficult due to medical advances (vaccines, diet changes, treatments). New conditions take time for sufficient claims data to build up.
- **Anti-Selection Risk**: Asymmetry of information (e.g., occupational exposure to cancer not picked up by UW). Mitigated by waiting periods (e.g., 6 months for cancer-related claims).
#### Major CI Conditions (Core List—~90% of Claims)
- **Typically Include**: Cancer, coronary artery bypass surgery, heart attack, kidney failure, major organ transplant, multiple sclerosis, stroke.

- **Definition Precision Example (Heart Attack)**: Death of heart muscle due to inadequate blood supply requiring: typical symptoms (chest pain), characteristic EKG changes, specific enzyme/Troponin levels. Excludes other acute coronary syndromes and angina.
#### Additional CI Conditions

- **Non-Major Conditions** (variable by market): Alzheimer's disease, AIDS/HIV (via transfusion or occupation), aorta graft surgery, benign brain tumour, blindness, coma, deafness, heart valve replacement/repair, loss of limbs, loss of speech, motor neurone disease, paralysis/paraplegia, severe burns, severe head injury.

- **Core vs Additional Model**: Some markets split conditions into 'Core' and 'Additional'; others use limited Core conditions (cancer, heart attack, stroke) with insurer-added options. Some markets standardise wording across insurers for consistency and faster settlement.
#### Risks & Claims Issues
- **Definition Disputes**: Complex medical terminology increases dispute risk. Precise wording essential to avoid litigation.
- **Earlier Diagnosis Trend**: Medical advances enable earlier diagnosis (sometimes before severe deterioration). Shifts claim timing; impacts frequency/severity balance.
- **Screening Effect**: Screening programmes may prompt claims that might have otherwise lapsed.
- **Limited Historical Data**: New markets lack sufficient experience data; proxy from reinsurers or population data (adjusted for insured population selectivity).
- **Moral Hazard**: Reduced compared to IP due to lump-sum nature; monitoring of recoveries still important.
#### Rider vs Standalone Capital Considerations
- **Standalone CI**: Separate pricing/reserving; own capital assessment; distinct profitability analysis.
- **Accelerated CI**: Rider on life cover; interacts with term life capital requirements; reduced sum at risk if CI paid before death.
#### Market Standardisation Benefits
- **Industry-Wide Wording**: Insurers/reinsurers cooperate on condition definitions; reduces ambiguity; faster claims settlement; fewer disputes.
- **Easier Comparisons**: Customers understand products; sales staff explain clearly; better industry-wide data collection.
- **Lower Risk Loading**: Better information allows lower premiums, increasing market size and profitability.
Income Protection: ### Income Protection (IP) Insurance Framework (SP1 Chapter 1)
#### Definition & Nature

- **Core Purpose**: Long-term insurance contract providing regular (short- or long-term) payments during periods of incapacity due to illness or accident. Returns regular premiums; multiple claims possible during policy term.
- **Complexity**: Seen as complex product; general policy conditions must be clear.
- **Key Distinction**: Unlike CI (fixed lump sum) or PMI (reimburse medical costs), IP replaces lost earnings. Benefit amount tied to pre-disability income.
#### Premium Structure & Guarantees
- **Premium Waiver**: Waived during periods when benefits payable.
- **Premium Escalation**: Subject to increases in line with regular benefit increases (e.g., earnings-linked benefit escalation).
- **No Claims Discounts (NCD)**: Reduced/waived premiums if no IP claims made during period.
- **Guaranteed Premiums**: Fixed throughout policy term (no changes).
- **Reviewable Premiums**: Changed on review dates only as result of portfolio experience (not individual experience); must be specified in policy.
#### Benefit Design & Restrictions
- **Replacement Ratio**: Benefit divided by pre-disability income (net of tax on both). Usually capped max 50-75% to maintain work incentive; over-insurance prevented via maximum benefit formula.
- **Benefit Duration**: Fixed term or up to specified age (usually retirement, e.g., age 65). Varying duration structures available.
- **Benefit Escalation at Outset**: Fixed percentage or published price/earnings index from start; often with corresponding premium increases.
- **Escalation During Claim**: Specified rate applied while benefits payable (maintains claimant's standard of living).
- **Partial/Proportionate Benefits**: Reduced-rate benefit if policyholder returns to part-time work or lower-paid job.
#### Definition of Incapacity
- **Core Definition**: Unable to earn income as result of illness or accident. Does NOT cover unemployment, redundancy, early retirement, reluctance to return to work.
- **Occupational Definitions**:
- Inability to perform own occupation
- Inability to perform own occupation + any other suited occupation (by education, status, training)
- Own occupation for initial period (e.g., 2 years), then any occupation thereafter
- Inability to perform any occupation
- **Functional/Non-Occupational Definitions**: Regardless of occupation—
- Activities of Daily Living (ADLs)
- Activities of Daily Working (ADWs)
- Functional Assessment Tests (FATs)
- Personal Capability Assessments (PCAs)
- **Use for Non-Earners**: Functional tests used for housepersons, students, temporarily unemployed.
#### Deferred Period & Linked-Claim Period
- **Deferred Period (DP)**: Chosen by policyholder; no benefits payable during this period (e.g., 4, 8, 13, 26 weeks). Longer DP = lower premium.
- **Linked-Claim Period**: Imposed by insurer (e.g., 4 weeks). Periods of incapacity separated by less than linked period treated as one continuous claim.
#### Options & Riders

- **Guaranteed Increase Options (GIOs)**: Increase benefit amount without further health evidence if 'life event' occurs (e.g., marriage, children, home purchase), provided opted at outset with extra premium.
- **Increase Options (General)**: Regular benefit increases guaranteed or reviewable.
#### Risk & Rating Factors
- **Typical Rating Factors**: Age, gender (if permitted), occupation (risk classes), deferred period, smoking status, medical history.
- **Location**: Typically not used as rating factor (despite evidence of effect); residency restrictions may apply for full cover.
- **Change of Occupation/Residence**: Policyholder must inform insurer of changes; may trigger premium adjustment or cover modification.
#### Product Variations
- **With-Profits IP**: Annual bonus declared, builds to maturity benefit on death/expiry.
- **Unit-Linked IP**: Morbidity charge deductions made from unit fund.

- **Short-Term Accident & Sickness (ASU) Policies**: Annual renewable; benefit payments limited to specified period (e.g., 1 year); often used for loan protection ('creditor insurance', 'payment protection insurance').
#### Group IP Specifics
- **Benefit Level**: Based on gross salary (2/3 to 3/4 typical) or net pay schemes (up to 90% net pre-disability income).
- **Additional Benefits**: Pension contributions (employee/employer), State welfare contributions.
- **Escalation in Claim**: While benefits paid, escalate to maintain claimant living standards.

- **Continuation Option**: Employees can convert group cover to individual without health evidence on leaving employment; anti-selection risk high (left due to health); benefits capped to group level.
- **Statutory Sick Pay**: In jurisdictions with statutory sick pay (e.g., UK 4 days–28 weeks), employers insure liability; deferred period often matches statutory period (e.g., 28 weeks DP in UK).
#### Key Risks for Underwriting/Pricing
- **Moral Hazard**: Incentive to remain disabled if replacement ratio too high; return-to-work incentives essential.
- **Economic/Unemployment Disguise**: Claim rates surge during economic downturns; unemployment masquerades as incapacity.
- **Definition Risk**: Own-occ vs any-occ disputes; occupational changes may shift claim likelihood.
- **Lapse Risk**: Policyholder may terminate if income improves or family circumstances change.
- **Claims Management Importance**: Early intervention, proportionate benefits for partial return to work, rehabilitation funding.
Pricing & Rating: - **Age/Gender**: Classic variables (though gender restricted in EU).
- **Experience Rating**: Adjusting renewal premiums based on past group claims.
- **NCD**: No Claims Discounts (common in PMI) to prevent trivial claims but hurts retention if lost.
- **Reviewability**: Guaranteed (fixed) vs Reviewable (can hike rates if experience worsens over the block).
State Healthcare & Demographics: - **State Role**: Universal coverage (PMJAY / NHS), means-tested social care.
- **Private Role**: Que-jumping, luxury facilities, covering non-critical procedures.
- **Demographics**: Aging populations increase LTC and medical costs, shrinking taxpayer bases.
- **Healthcare Economics**: QALYs (Quality Adjusted Life Years) to decide drug funding via agencies like NICE.
Long-Term Care (LTC): ### Long-Term Care Insurance Framework (SP1 Chapter 3)
#### Definition & Scope

- **Core Definition**: All forms of continuing personal or nursing care and associated domestic services for people unable to care for themselves without support. Provided in own homes, day centres, or State-sponsored/private care homes.
- **Distinct from Acute Care**: Designed for people NOT expected to recover. Focuses on day-to-day living (getting up/bed, washing, cooking, shopping) rather than curing medical conditions.
- **Applies to All Ages**: While SP1 focuses on elderly, LTCI benefits payable for care need at any age (disabled from birth, accident, illness).
- **Informal vs Formal Care**: Informal (family/spouse) has indirect cost (lost economic activity, replacement care cost). Formal provided at home or residential care.
#### Cost Components (Must Separate)
- **Living Costs**: Food, clothing, heating, amenities (extra needed for care needs).
- **Housing Costs** ('Hotel' Element): Rent, mortgage, council tax, accommodation adaptations (bath hoist). Higher for care recipients.
- **Personal Care Costs**: Additional costs from frailty/disability; narrower 'nursing care' (qualified nurse knowledge/skills) more expensive than other personal care.
- **Policy Approach**: Typically cover EXTRA costs (difference from non-care scenario); not full costs.
#### Long-Term Care Provision
- **Formal Care Settings**: Majority home-based (preferred by individuals, government policy); private/charity residential homes for others.
- **Care Delivery**: Private care providers, nursing homes, care homes run by charities (serve specific communities).
- **Intermediate Care**: Recuperative services post-acute event (heart attack, stroke, accident) to prevent hospital readmission; bridges hospital-home gap.
#### Uncertainties in Care Needs Assessment
- **Likelihood of Need**: Medical advances cure previously debilitating illnesses; genetic predisposition variable; published statistics may not apply to individuals.
- **Extent of Care Required**: Depends on medical advances, technological advances (equipment enabling independence), speed of deterioration, availability of informal support.
- **Duration of Care**: Longevity unknown; life expectancy based on averages, not individual factors; mortality improvements increase uncertainty.
- **Risk**: If underestimated, accumulated wealth insufficient to meet care needs.
#### Types of LTCI Products

- **Pre-Funded Plans**: Purchased by relatively healthy people to protect against future disability. Secured by single premium at outset or level annual premiums throughout life (or to specified age). Premium waiver during claim.
- **Immediate Needs Plans**: Purchased by long-term care claimants (already needing care). Single-premium product; benefit payable from start of care.
- **Indemnity-Based**: Ideally reimburses additional costs of day-to-day living when in need of care. May subject to overriding maxima to control claims costs.
- **Cash Benefit Products**: Not indemnity; provide fixed cash benefits (insurer knows exposure).
#### Benefit Triggers & Structure
- **Trigger Mechanism**: Benefit payable on entry to care (ADL loss: Activities of Daily Living loss; e.g., unable to bathe, toilet, dress without assistance).
- **Multiple Dependency Levels**: Independent → Partial Dependency → Full Dependency. Transfers between levels tracked; potential for down-grade (improvement) and up-grade (deterioration).
- **Benefit Escalation**: Long-term contracts need inflation-protection. Real assets (equities) for inflation match; investment importance significant (large reserves).
#### Product Design Considerations
- **Deferred Period**: Deferred period reduces risk-premium. Longer DP = lower cost (e.g., 0, 13, 26, 52 weeks). Policyholder pays care costs during DP from own resources.
- **Surrender/Discontinuance Values**: Savings element justifies surrender value design (unlike pure protection IP/CI). Return-of-premium rider possible.
- **Premium Guarantee**: Long-term contracts offered both guaranteed and reviewable variants.
- **Funding Mechanism**: Single premium or level annual premiums (spreads cost; amortization risk if lapse); premium waiver during claim.
#### Complementarity with Other Products
- **CI Insurance Overlap**: Many critical illnesses in old age lead to LTCI need. Good overlap but distinct triggers (CI: diagnosis; LTCI: ADL loss).
- **PMI Complement**: LTCI benefits similar to PMI but cover longer-term chronic care; PMI covers acute treatment. Different benefit profiles.

- **IP Insurance**: IP + LTCI combination covers earnings loss (IP) + care costs if retirement (LTCI). No link between IP benefit level (earnings-based) and LTCI benefit (care-cost-based); problems in integrated design.
- **Life Insurance**: Good overlap (care benefits viewed as 'longevity insurance'). LTCI often added as rider to life cover.
- **Bundling Risk**: Adding LTCI (low consumer interest) to CI/life (high consumer interest) increases attachment; complicates experience analysis, claims management, capital assessment.
#### Risks & Underwriting
- **High Claim Inception**: LTCI has high per-capita claim rates compared to life insurance.
- **Long Duration**: Average claims duration 3-5 years; significant reserve build.
- **Moral Hazard**: Reduced (fixed benefit); but monitoring needed for claim verification/duration.
- **Anti-Selection**: Perception of '100% care coverage' attracts those expecting to need care. Underwriting at entry critical.
- **Medical Advances**: Can prolong life in disability (increasing costs) or hasten recovery (reducing costs); unpredictable.
- **Dependency Tracking**: Detailed record-keeping on dependency level transitions (up-grade/down-grade).
#### Key Actuarial Challenges
- **Large Reserves**: Significant financial impact due to high inception/duration; investment importance high (real asset allocation for inflation, fixed-interest for guarantees).
- **Limited Data**: New markets lack historical experience; proxy from reinsurers or population data (adjusted for insured selectivity).
- **Pricing Complexity**: Must model claim inception rates, duration by dependency level, transitions between levels, inflation impact.
- **Capital Requirements**: LTCI strains capital at launch (negative asset share early); significant SCR impact due to guarantees/policyholder options.
Legislation & Taxation: - **Premiums/Benefits**: Are premiums tax deductible? Are benefits tax free? Drives product demand.
- **Employer Tax**: Incentivizes Group PMI / Group IP if treated favorably as business expense.
- **Anti-Discrimination**: Equality acts preventing pricing based on genetics, gender, or disabilities without pure statistical proof.
Product Design (Protection): - **Standalone vs Riders**: Selling CI or Death cover attached to a savings/mortgage plan.
- **Unit-Linked**: Charging mortality/morbidity costs by deducting units from a fund (reviewable by nature).
- **Options**: Conversion options, Guaranteed Insurability Options (GIOs) without medicals.
Administration: - **Setup Constraints**: Can legacy IT handle complex tiered benefits or unit-deductions?
- **Customer Journey**: Quotation portals, premium collection methods (Direct Debit).
- **Outsourcing**: Using Third Party Administrators (TPAs) for claims, call centers. Retaining oversight.
Monitoring: - **Analysis of Experience**: Systematic comparison of actual to expected (A/E ratios) by assumption type: morbidity incidence rates; claim duration and recovery rates; mortality rates (pre-claim and impaired); lapse and persistency rates; expense ratios (acquisition, administration, claim management); investment returns. Data segmented into homogeneous groups by age, gender, occupation, smoker status, region, and duration before analysis. Credibility weighting applied where own data is thin — blend actual with reinsurer, market, or population data (adjusted for relevance). Key outputs: identify systematic bias (model error) vs random fluctuation; detect trends (improving mortality, rising incidence, medical inflation); trigger assumption updates in pricing and valuation. For IP business — investigate higher inception rates, lower recovery rates, higher claim amounts, changes in business mix, product terms, economic conditions (recession increases claims and lapses), and outliers/large schemes. For PMI — investigate claims frequency and severity separately; medical inflation vs embedded assumption; changes in provider pricing; state healthcare system changes; underwriting drift. For LTCI — geographical variation in cost of care; claims inflation; investment income; evolving business mix profile. For all products — actual experience compared with pricing assumptions and reasons for divergence identified; data on exposed-to-risk required alongside claims and lapses. [Key sittings: 202104 Q3(ii), 202409 Q3(i), 202204 Q2(i)/(ii)]

- **Analysis of Surplus or Profit**: Reconciliation of actual surplus emerging in a period to that expected under the valuation basis; broken down into component sources enabling appropriate decisions on distribution. Sources identified separately: mortality/morbidity surplus (actual vs expected claims); persistency surplus (actual vs expected lapses — selective lapses worsen risk pool); expense surplus (actual vs expected — split acquisition, admin, claims management, inflation); investment surplus (actual vs expected return); new business strain (reserves exceed premiums at outset — requires capital); release of prudential margins (margins unwinding as policies run off); change of basis (effect of assumption or methodology changes on reserves — e.g. introduction of new data source or improved segmentation may reduce reserves and release surplus); interest on opening surplus. Uses of surplus: augment available capital (proprietary company); cover new business strain or fund business growth; repay securitisation loans; reduce reinsurance usage; acquire blocks of business from other insurers; distribute as dividend; retain as buffer if surplus arose from uncertain methodology changes. Without-profits business — 100% of surplus attributable to shareholders. [Key sittings: 202209 Q2(iv)/(v)/(vi), 202009 Q3(iii)]

- **Analysis of EV**: Reconciliation of opening to closing Embedded Value over a period. Standard components: opening EV; expected return on EV (unwinding of discount at risk discount rate); value of new business (VNB) written in the period; experience variances (actual vs assumed mortality, lapses, expenses, investment return); assumption changes (effect of updating long-term assumptions on VIF); economic variances (changes in risk-free rates, equity returns); other operating variances; closing EV. VNB is the primary new business metric — present value of future after-tax profits on new policies written after deducting cost of required capital; VNB margin = VNB divided by APE or PVNBP. Sensitivity analysis published: typically showing impact of ±1% risk discount rate, ±10% mortality, ±10% lapses. Used by management to assess which products, channels, and geographies create shareholder value. Links to experience analysis — experience variances in EV analysis reconcile to the same drivers investigated in experience analysis (incidence, lapse, expense, investment). [Key sittings: 202009 Q3(iii), 201909 Q1(vii)/(viii)]
Reporting: - **Solvency II**: European prudential framework; three-pillar structure. Pillar 1 (quantitative capital): two capital requirements — SCR (Solvency Capital Requirement) at 99.5% VaR over one year; MCR (Minimum Capital Requirement) as absolute floor, calculated by simple factor-based linear formula at 85% VaR, floored at 25% and capped at 45% of SCR; absolute minimum €3.7m (long-term) or €2.5m (short-term); SCR calculated via standard formula or approved internal model; balance sheet on market-consistent basis; technical provisions = Best Estimate Liabilities (BEL) + Risk Margin (RM); BEL split into premium provisions (future risk exposure) and claims provisions (incurred claims); discount rate reflects risk-free rates with optional matching adjustment or volatility adjustment; Risk Margin based on 6% cost-of-capital applied to projected non-hedgeable risks; reinsurance recoverables shown gross on liability side, recoverable as asset. Pillar 2 (governance and ORSA): Own Risk and Solvency Assessment — insurer identifies risks (including those not in Pillar 1), risk management processes, and ongoing ability to meet MCR and SCR; supervisory review process; capital add-ons if risks inadequately modelled. Pillar 3 (disclosure): Solvency and Financial Condition Report (SFCR) public disclosure; Regular Supervisory Report (RSR) to regulator; Quantitative Reporting Templates (QRTs). Standard formula vs internal model: appropriateness assessed risk module by risk module via ORSA; for health insurer — insurance risk (premium and reserve risk) significant; lapse and catastrophe risks less material for short-term PMI; standard formula likely fits if risk profile does not materially deviate. Group supervision: group SCR allows diversification; group solvency floor = sum of solo MCRs; addresses double use of capital and double leverage; third-country equivalence (Bermuda and Switzerland — full; Japan, Australia, USA — temporary). [Key sittings: 202004 Q1(i)/(ii), 201904 Q2(i)/(ii)/(iii)/(iv), 202009 Q1(v)]

- **Risk Based Capital**: Moves away from fixed factor-based solvency (current Indian regime: ASM ≥ 150% RSM, where RSM uses fixed percentages of premiums and claims regardless of actual risk profile) toward capital requirements explicitly linked to each insurer's own risk exposures. Key features: capital derived from underwriting risk, market risk, credit risk, operational risk, and liquidity risk; diversification benefits between risk categories recognised; insurer-specific rather than industry-uniform; encourages better internal risk modelling and management. SCR under Solvency II is the primary international RBC model — standard formula provides prescribed stress factors per risk module; internal model permitted if approved by regulator and demonstrably better reflects risk profile. Indian transition: IRDAI developing RBC framework alongside Ind AS 117; current RSM formula does not differentiate between a well-diversified low-risk insurer and a concentrated high-risk one — RBC corrects this. Key exam angle: regulator concern that holding only minimum solvency is insufficient — SCR breach expected once in 200 years vs MCR breach expected every 6–7 years; holding minimum provides no buffer against even moderate adverse experience; regulator may force cessation of new business or merger if breach occurs. [Key sittings: 202009 Q1(v), 201904 Q2(iv), 202004 Q1(i)]

- **IFRS 17**: Replaces IFRS 4; effective for annual periods beginning on or after 1 January 2023; Indian equivalent is Ind AS 117. Groups insurance contracts into: onerous (day-one loss recognised immediately in P&L — no CSM); no significant risk of becoming onerous; remaining. Three measurement models: General Measurement Model (GMM/BBA) — building blocks are Present Value of Future Cash Flows (PVFCF) + Risk Adjustment (RA) for non-financial risk + Contractual Service Margin (CSM); Premium Allocation Approach (PAA) — simplified model for short-duration contracts (coverage period ≤1 year or where results approximate GMM — most PMI and short-term health products qualify); Variable Fee Approach (VFA) — for contracts with direct participation features (unit-linked). CSM represents unearned profit at inception, released to P&L over coverage period in proportion to coverage units as service is provided; tracked separately per group of contracts. RA reflects compensation the insurer requires for bearing uncertainty in amount and timing of cash flows from non-financial risk. Discount rates reflect characteristics of the insurance liability, not insurer's assets. Insurance revenue ≠ premium received — revenue recognised as insurance service delivered; acquisition cash flows included in measurement. Reinsurance contracts held measured separately — cannot be offset against underlying. Transition approaches: full retrospective; modified retrospective; fair value approach. Link to Analysis of Surplus: CSM movement analysis under IFRS 17 is the equivalent of the traditional analysis of surplus — experience adjustments, assumption changes, and new business CSM recognised each period. [Key sittings: not yet directly examined — foundational knowledge required]

- **Embedded Value**: Measure of consolidated value of shareholders' interests in an in-force life (and long-term health) insurance business.
